chapter_context.summary: Nathan rebukes David using a parable of a rich man stealing a poor man's lamb; David repents; their child dies; Solomon is born; Joab takes Rabbah.

outline.text: Nathan Rebukes David with the Parable of the Ewe Lamb
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Nathan Rebukes David with the Parable of the Ewe Lamb (12:1-6), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

outline.text: Nathan Pronounces Judgment; David Confesses and is Forgiven
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Nathan Pronounces Judgment; David Confesses and is Forgiven (12:7-15), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

outline.text: The Sickness and Death of Bathsheba's Firstborn
outline.explanation: This section focuses on The Sickness and Death of Bathsheba's Firstborn (12:16-23), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.
This section focuses on The Birth of Solomon (Jedidiah) (12:24-25), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

outline.text: Joab Takes Rabbah; David Spoil the Ammonites
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Joab Takes Rabbah; David Spoil the Ammonites (12:26-31), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

key_themes.title: The Davidic Covenant
key_themes.description: God's unconditional promise of an eternal dynasty, ultimately pointing forward to the Messiah.

key_themes.title: Sin and Consequences
key_themes.description: While God offers complete forgiveness for sincere repentance, the earthly consequences of sin remain.

devotional.question: How do the events of Chapter 12 demonstrate the grace of God in the midst of human failure?
What lessons about leadership and accountability can you apply to your own life from this chapter?

cultural_historical.title: Jerusalem as Capital
cultural_historical.text: By capturing Jerusalem, a neutral city on the border of Judah and Benjamin, David strategically united the fractured northern and southern tribes under a single capital.
